name: Basil
tagline: The most rewarding herb on a windowsill. Smells incredible, grows fast, makes everything taste better.

water.detail: Let top 1cm of soil dry out. Wilting leaves = thirsty plant.

sun.detail: South-facing windowsill is ideal. Less sun = leggy, flavourless leaves.

soil.detail: Regular potting mix. Avoid heavy garden soil.

temp.detail: Hates cold. Never below 10°C.

seedDepth.detail: Just barely cover seeds. Sprouts in 5–10 days.

tips: Pinch off flower buds immediately — once basil flowers the leaves turn bitter.
Pick from the top down, always leave 2 sets of leaves on the stem.
Water at the base, not the leaves. Wet leaves invite mildew.
Grow near tomatoes — they genuinely do better together.
warning: Don't put basil near a cold draught or AC vent. It will turn black overnight.

name: Tomato
tagline: More work than most but a warm tomato from your own garden is worth every bit of it.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist. Irregular watering causes fruit to split.

sun.detail: Less than 6 hours = lots of leaves, barely any fruit.

soil.detail: Mix compost in. Feed every 2 weeks once fruiting starts.

temp.detail: Won't set fruit above 35°C or below 10°C.

seedDepth.detail: Sow indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost.

tips: Bury the stem deep when transplanting — roots grow all along the buried stem.
Pinch out 'suckers' in the V between main stem and branch.
Yellow lower leaves are normal — remove for better airflow.
Crushed eggshells in the planting hole prevent blossom end rot.
warning: Blossom end rot (black patch on fruit bottom) means inconsistent watering — not disease.

name: Garden Peas
tagline: Sweet fresh peas from the garden taste nothing like anything from a shop.

water.detail: Keep moist especially when pods are forming.

sun.detail: Full sun preferred but tolerates partial shade.

soil.detail: Not too rich — high nitrogen means lots of leaves, few pods.

temp.detail: Cool weather crop. Struggles in summer heat above 25°C.

seedDepth.detail: Sow direct in the ground. Space seeds 5–7cm apart.

care.spacing: 5–7 cm in row, rows 45 cm apart

tips: Push twiggy sticks or a trellis in when you sow — peas climb immediately.
Pick pods regularly — leaving pods on signals the plant to stop producing.
Sow in batches every 2–3 weeks for a continuous harvest.
After harvesting, cut stems at ground level and leave roots — they feed the next crop.
warning: Mice and birds love freshly sown pea seeds. Cover with netting for the first 2–3 weeks.

name: Carrot
tagline: Slow and satisfying. Pulling your first homegrown carrot is unexpectedly joyful.

water.detail: Keep evenly moist. Uneven watering causes roots to split.

sun.detail: Full sun produces the best, sweetest roots.

soil.detail: Compacted or stony soil causes forked, stubby carrots.

temp.detail: Light frost actually sweetens the roots.

seedDepth.detail: Tiny seeds — sprinkle thinly in a shallow drill. Cover lightly.

tips: Thin seedlings ruthlessly when 5cm tall — crowded carrots are tiny and forked.
Don't add fresh compost before sowing — it causes forked roots.
Sow a pinch of radish seeds with carrots — they germinate fast and mark the row.
Green carrot shoulders taste bitter — mound soil up over them as they grow.
warning: Carrot fly is the main pest. Use fleece or a 60cm barrier around the bed.

name: Cucumber
tagline: Fast-growing and incredibly productive. One plant can produce more than a family can eat.

water.detail: Cucumbers are 95% water — inconsistent watering causes bitter fruit.

sun.detail: Warmth and sun are essential.

soil.detail: Dig in plenty of compost. Mulch around the base.

temp.detail: Don't plant outside until nights are above 12°C.

seedDepth.detail: Sow on their side to prevent rotting. Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost.

tips: Train up a trellis — vertical growing gives straighter fruit and saves space.
Pick when full length but before turning yellow — yellow ones are overripe.
Pinch out the growing tip once it reaches the top of its support.
Feed with high-potassium liquid feed every week once flowering starts.
warning: Powdery mildew appears as white powder on leaves in late summer. Improve airflow and avoid wetting leaves.

name: Spinach
tagline: Incredibly fast from seed. You can be eating salad leaves within 3–4 weeks of sowing.

water.detail: Never let it dry out — stress causes it to bolt immediately.

sun.detail: One of the best crops for shadier spots.

soil.detail: Spinach is a hungry plant. Add plenty of compost.

temp.detail: Cool weather crop. Bolts quickly in heat above 25°C.

seedDepth.detail: Sow direct in rows. Thin to 15cm apart.

tips: Sow every 3 weeks from early spring to early summer, then again in late summer.
Harvest outer leaves first, leaving the growing centre.
In summer heat, grow under light shade netting.
If it bolts, your garden is getting too warm for cool-weather crops.
warning: Bolts extremely fast in hot weather. Once it bolts, leaves turn bitter and the plant is done.

name: Kale
tagline: The toughest vegetable you can grow. Frost improves the flavour. Grows when nothing else will.

water.detail: Keep moist but not waterlogged.

sun.detail: Full sun preferred but handles partial shade.

soil.detail: Kale loves rich compost. Firm the soil well after planting.

temp.detail: Frost genuinely improves sweetness.

seedDepth.detail: Sow direct or start in trays 6 weeks before transplanting.

tips: Harvest lower, outer leaves and leave the central growing point — produces for months.
After frost, let harvested leaves sit a day before cooking — starches convert to sugars.
Curly kale and Cavolo Nero are the easiest varieties for beginners.
Check undersides of leaves weekly for caterpillar eggs and squash them.
warning: Rotate where you grow kale each year — don't grow it in the same spot twice (clubroot disease).

name: Lettuce
tagline: The fastest, easiest food you can grow. Seed to salad in under 30 days.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist. Drying out makes it bitter and causes bolting.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade. In hot weather, shade prevents bolting.

soil.detail: Mix in plenty of compost.

temp.detail: Bolts quickly in heat above 25°C.

seedDepth.detail: Seeds need light to germinate. Press lightly onto moist soil.

tips: Cut-and-come-again: harvest outer leaves, leave the centre. Keeps producing for weeks.
Sow small amounts every 2–3 weeks for continuous supply.
In summer, sow in afternoon shade — extends harvest before it bolts.
Water in the morning so leaves dry during the day, reducing mildew.
warning: Bolts in heat or drought. In summer, shade cloth is essential.

name: Courgette / Zucchini
tagline: Outrageously productive. One plant can overwhelm a family with courgettes in summer.

water.detail: Large leaves lose water fast. Consistent watering prevents bitter fruit.

sun.detail: Loves heat and sun. Won't perform in a cool shadowy spot.

soil.detail: Dig in a full bucket of compost per plant.

temp.detail: Cold-sensitive. Even a light frost kills young plants.

seedDepth.detail: Sow seeds on their edge to prevent rotting.

tips: Pick every 2–3 days — a missed courgette becomes a marrow overnight.
If fruit rots at the end, hand-pollinate with a brush between male and female flowers.
Feed with high-potassium liquid feed every 2 weeks once flowering starts.
One plant is usually enough for a family. Two is ambitious.
warning: Powdery mildew appears from midsummer — normal and rarely fatal. Improve airflow.

name: Bell Pepper
tagline: Takes patience but growing your own peppers — especially red ones — is deeply satisfying.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist. Irregular watering causes fruit to drop.

sun.detail: More sun = more fruit and faster ripening to red.

soil.detail: Mix in compost. Feed weekly with tomato fertiliser once fruiting starts.

temp.detail: Very heat-loving. Never plant outside until nights are above 15°C.

seedDepth.detail: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost. Germination needs warmth (24°C+).

tips: Start seeds indoors as early as January/February — peppers need a long season.
Green peppers are just unripe red/yellow peppers. Leave them on longer for full sweetness.
Stake the plants early — heavy fruit can snap branches.
Grow in a greenhouse or the sunniest, most sheltered spot you have.
warning: Peppers are slow and demand heat. In cool climates, a greenhouse or polytunnel makes the difference between a harvest and disappointment.

name: Onion
tagline: Incredibly easy when grown from sets (small bulbs). Low maintenance and stores for months after harvest.

water.detail: Don't overwater. Onions prefer drier conditions than most vegetables.

sun.detail: Full sun essential. Poor sun = small bulbs.

soil.detail: Add compost but avoid fresh manure — causes disease.

temp.detail: Hardy. Plant sets in early spring or autumn depending on variety.

seedDepth.detail: Plant sets (small bulbs) with the tip just showing above soil. From seed: 1cm deep.

tips: Grow from sets (small onion bulbs) rather than seed — much easier and faster.
Stop watering when the leaves start to flop over — this is the signal to harvest.
Lift bulbs and let them dry in the sun for 2 weeks before storing.
Never store onions near potatoes — they make each other rot faster.
warning: Onion fly and white rot are the main problems. Rotate crops every year and never grow onions in the same bed two years running.

name: Garlic
tagline: Plant in autumn, forget about it all winter, harvest in summer. One of the most hands-off vegetables you can grow.

water.detail: Water only in very dry spells. Overwatering causes rot.

sun.detail: Full sun for the best bulbs.

soil.detail: Garlic hates sitting in wet soil. Raised beds or sandy loam ideal.

temp.value: Needs cold period to bulb up
temp.detail: Plant in autumn — it needs winter cold (vernalisation) to form proper bulbs.

seedDepth.detail: Plant individual cloves pointy-end up, 5–7cm deep.

tips: Plant in October/November for a July harvest. Spring-planted garlic produces much smaller bulbs.
Harvest when the bottom 3–4 leaves have turned brown but the top leaves are still green.
Let bulbs cure in a warm dry place for 3–4 weeks before storing — they'll last months.
Save the biggest cloves from your harvest to plant next year — you'll gradually select for your conditions.
warning: Rust (orange streaks on leaves) is common and rarely fatal. Improve airflow and don't grow alliums in the same spot two years in a row.

name: Potato
tagline: Incredibly satisfying to grow. Digging up potatoes feels like finding buried treasure every time.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist especially once flowers appear — this is when tubers swell.

sun.detail: Full sun essential for good yields.

soil.detail: Deep loose soil lets tubers expand freely. Dig in plenty of compost.

temp.detail: Frost kills the green tops. If a late frost threatens, earth up the stems.

seedDepth.detail: Plant seed potatoes 10–15cm deep. Earth up as they grow.

tips: Chit (pre-sprout) seed potatoes on a windowsill for 4–6 weeks before planting for earlier harvest.
Earth up the stems every few weeks — mound soil over the emerging shoots to increase yield.
New potatoes: harvest when flowers open. Maincrop: wait until foliage dies back completely.
Never eat green potatoes — the green parts contain solanine which is toxic.
warning: Blight is the main risk in warm wet summers — brown patches on leaves spread fast. Remove affected foliage immediately and don't compost it.

name: Broccoli
tagline: Homegrown broccoli is sweeter and more tender than anything from a shop.

water.detail: Consistent moisture. Dry spells cause small, poor-quality heads.

sun.detail: Full sun essential.

soil.detail: Firm soil is important — brassicas need stability. Add lime if soil is acidic.

temp.detail: Cool weather crop. Heads bolt and flower quickly in heat.

seedDepth.detail: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting.

tips: Harvest the central head before any yellow flowers open — once it flowers it's over.
After cutting the main head, smaller side shoots continue to produce for weeks.
Net plants to protect from cabbage white butterfly caterpillars.
Don't grow in the same spot as other brassicas from the previous year.
warning: Cabbage white butterfly caterpillars can strip plants overnight. Check undersides of leaves weekly.

name: Radish
tagline: The fastest vegetable you can grow. Seed to harvest in under 4 weeks. Perfect for impatient gardeners.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist. Irregular watering causes hollow, woody roots.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade better than most vegetables.

soil.detail: Light soil allows roots to develop properly.

temp.detail: Cool weather crop. Bolts and turns hot and pithy in summer heat.

seedDepth.detail: Sow direct. Thin to 5cm apart.

tips: Harvest promptly when ready — left too long they turn woody, hollow, and very hot.
Sow a short row every week from spring to early summer for constant supply.
Sow alongside slow-germinating crops like carrots — radishes mark the row and loosen the soil.
In summer, switch to slower-maturing daikon (mooli) radishes which tolerate heat better.
warning: Flea beetle makes tiny holes in the leaves — use fleece or fine mesh to protect seedlings.

name: Beetroot
tagline: Incredibly versatile and beautiful. The deep red leaves look as good as the roots taste.

water.detail: Keep moist but not waterlogged. Drought causes tough, woody roots.

sun.detail: Full sun preferred but tolerates light shade.

soil.detail: Too much nitrogen means lots of leaves and tiny roots.

temp.detail: Fairly adaptable. Tolerates light frost.

seedDepth.detail: Each 'seed' is actually a cluster — thin to one seedling after germination.

tips: Each seed cluster produces multiple seedlings — thin to the strongest one.
Don't discard the thinnings — the baby leaves are delicious in salads.
Harvest when golf-ball to tennis-ball sized — they get tough and woody if left too long.
The leaves are edible too, cooked like chard.
warning: Bolts (runs to seed) quickly if sown too early in cold soil. Wait until soil is above 10°C.

name: Sweetcorn
tagline: Sweetcorn eaten within minutes of picking is a completely different vegetable from anything in a shop.

water.detail: Needs consistent moisture especially when silks appear and cobs are forming.

sun.detail: Full sun and warmth essential.

soil.detail: Dig in plenty of compost. Feed monthly with general fertiliser.

temp.detail: Needs warm soil (above 16°C). Very cold-sensitive.

seedDepth.detail: Start indoors in small pots 3–4 weeks before last frost.

tips: Plant in blocks of at least 4×4, not in rows — sweetcorn is wind-pollinated and needs neighbours to set cobs.
Test for ripeness: peel back the husk and pierce a kernel with a fingernail. Milky liquid = ready. Clear = not yet. Doughy = overripe.
Cook within minutes of picking — the sugars convert to starch extremely fast.
Grow a second sowing 2–3 weeks after the first for an extended harvest.
warning: Birds and squirrels will dig up germinating seeds. Cover with fleece until plants are 15cm tall.

name: Pumpkin
tagline: Grows enormous from a single seed and keeps for months after harvest. A dramatic garden plant.

water.detail: Enormous leaves lose water fast. Never let them wilt.

sun.detail: Needs maximum sun and warmth.

soil.detail: Plant on a mound of pure compost. They are the hungriest plants in the garden.

temp.detail: Cold-sensitive. Plant out only when weather is settled and warm.

seedDepth.detail: Sow on edge to prevent rotting. Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost.

tips: Let the vines sprawl or train them — they can cover 3–4 square metres per plant.
Limit to 2–3 fruits per plant for the biggest results. Remove extra developing fruits.
Harvest when the skin is hard and the stem is starting to dry and cork over.
Cure after harvest in a warm dry spot for 2 weeks — this hardens the skin and extends storage to 6 months.
warning: Powdery mildew is inevitable by late summer. It's cosmetic — the plant is done producing by then anyway.

name: Aloe Vera
tagline: The world's most forgiving houseplant. Neglect it and it thrives. Overwater it and it dies.

water.detail: Water deeply then let soil dry completely. In winter, once a month.

sun.detail: Loves light but direct harsh sun scorches tips.

soil.detail: Drainage is everything. Add perlite if using regular potting mix.

temp.detail: Bring indoors if below 5°C.

seedDepth.detail: Separate 'pups' at the base and pot separately.

tips: Thin curling leaves = thirsty. Soft mushy base = overwatered.
Always use a pot with drainage holes. Standing water kills aloe.
Repot only when roots emerge from the bottom — aloe likes being root-bound.
The clear gel inside is real — slice a lower leaf open for burns or dry skin.
warning: Root rot from overwatering is almost always fatal. When in doubt, don't water.

name: Peace Lily
tagline: The most tolerant houseplant for low-light rooms. Droops when thirsty but recovers within an hour.

water.detail: Water when top 2–3cm is dry. It droops visibly when thirsty.

sun.detail: Survives quite dark rooms. Direct sun scorches leaves.

soil.detail: Standard houseplant mix. Repot every 1–2 years.

temp.detail: Hates cold draughts and below 12°C.

seedDepth.detail: Not grown from seed at home. Divide root ball when repotting.

tips: Brown leaf tips = air too dry. Put pot on a tray of damp pebbles.
Wipe leaves with damp cloth monthly — removes dust and helps the plant breathe.
Don't rush to repot — peace lilies flower better when slightly root-bound.
Genuinely filters indoor air pollutants including formaldehyde.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs if eaten. Wash hands after handling sap.

name: Monstera
tagline: The iconic Swiss cheese plant. Almost impossible to kill, grows impressively large.

water.detail: Water when top 2–5cm of soil is dry. Overwatering is the main mistake.

sun.detail: Thrives in bright filtered light. Direct sun scorches leaves.

soil.detail: Chunky potting mix with perlite. Roots need oxygen.

temp.detail: No cold draughts. Below 10°C causes damage.

seedDepth.detail: Take a cutting with at least one node, root in water or moist compost.

tips: Holes in the leaves are normal and healthy — they appear as the plant matures.
Wipe leaves monthly — large leaves accumulate a lot of dust.
Give it a moss pole — it grows bigger leaves with something to attach to.
Yellow leaves usually mean overwatering.
warning: All parts are toxic to cats, dogs and humans if ingested.

name: Snake Plant
tagline: The toughest houseplant alive. Survives weeks without water, thrives in low light.

water.detail: Let soil dry completely between waterings.

sun.detail: Survives very low light. Better growth in brighter spots.

soil.detail: Cactus mix or regular mix with added grit.

temp.detail: Tolerates a wide range. Avoid below 10°C.

seedDepth.detail: Cut a leaf into sections and push into gritty compost, or divide at the root.

tips: The only way to kill it is to overwater — root rot is fatal and fast.
Wipe leaves with a damp cloth to keep them glossy.
Produces oxygen at night — a popular choice for bedrooms.
Repot only every 2–3 years — it prefers being root-bound.
warning: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs. Can cause nausea if eaten.

name: Pothos
tagline: Practically grows itself. Trails beautifully from shelves, tolerates neglect, and propagates for free.

water.detail: Water when top 2–3cm is dry. Yellowing leaves usually mean overwatering.

sun.detail: Incredibly adaptable. The variegated varieties need more light to keep their pattern.

soil.detail: Standard potting mix with some perlite.

temp.detail: No cold draughts. Below 10°C causes leaf drop.

seedDepth.detail: Cut just below a node, place in water. Roots in 2–4 weeks then pot up.

tips: Propagate endlessly — put cuttings in a glass of water on the windowsill and pot them up when rooted.
Wipe leaves occasionally — they accumulate dust fast.
Pale, washed-out leaves in a variegated variety = not enough light.
A single plant can eventually trail several metres — great for high shelves.
warning: Toxic to cats and dogs. Can cause oral irritation if eaten.

name: Spider Plant
tagline: The most beginner-friendly houseplant. Produces baby plants constantly. Almost impossible to kill.

water.detail: Water when soil is partly dry. Very forgiving of irregular watering.

sun.detail: Bright indirect light is ideal. Tolerates lower light well.

soil.detail: Standard houseplant mix. Not fussy.

temp.detail: Very adaptable. Tolerates cool rooms but not frost.

seedDepth.detail: Pin the baby plants (spiderettes) onto moist compost. Roots form in 1–2 weeks.

tips: The hanging baby plants (spiderettes) can be pinned onto compost in a separate pot while still attached to the mother — snip the runner once rooted.
Brown leaf tips are common and usually caused by fluoride in tap water — use filtered or rainwater.
One pot can produce dozens of baby plants in a season — great for gifts.
Perfect for hanging baskets where the long trailing runners look spectacular.
warning: Non-toxic to humans and pets — one of the safest houseplants you can have.

name: Fiddle Leaf Fig
tagline: The most dramatic-looking houseplant you can own. Also the most demanding. Worth it if you can get it right.

water.detail: Water thoroughly, let the top 3–5cm dry out between waterings. Very sensitive to over and underwatering.

sun.detail: Needs consistent bright light. A few hours of gentle morning sun is ideal.

soil.detail: Use good quality potting mix with perlite. Must drain freely.

temp.detail: Hates temperature fluctuations. Keep away from vents, draughty windows, and outside doors.

seedDepth.detail: Take a large cutting with 2–3 leaves, dip in rooting hormone, keep warm and humid.

tips: Once you find a spot it's happy in — don't move it. It drops leaves every time it's moved.
Wipe each large leaf with a damp cloth monthly — dust blocks the light it needs.
Brown spots with yellow halo = overwatering. Brown crispy edges = underwatering or low humidity.
Feed monthly in spring and summer with a balanced liquid fertiliser.
warning: Drops leaves dramatically when stressed by moving, cold draughts, or sudden changes in light or watering. Patience is essential.

name: ZZ Plant
tagline: Practically thrives on neglect. Dark, dry rooms that kill everything else — the ZZ plant does fine.

water.detail: Stores water in its thick roots. Let soil dry completely between waterings.

sun.detail: One of the most shade-tolerant houseplants. Direct sun bleaches the leaves.

soil.detail: Cactus mix or standard mix with added grit.

temp.detail: Tolerates a wide range. Avoid below 8°C.

seedDepth.detail: Very slow to propagate — patience required. Division is faster.

tips: Water even less in winter — once a month is often enough.
The thick underground rhizomes store water — the plant is genuinely drought-adapted.
Wipe the glossy leaves monthly for best appearance.
Perfect for offices and rooms with artificial light only.
warning: All parts are toxic if ingested and the sap can irritate skin. Wear gloves when handling and keep away from children and pets.

name: Mint
tagline: Grows so aggressively the main challenge is stopping it taking over. Always grow in a pot.

water.detail: Likes moisture. Consistent watering = best flavour.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade better than most herbs.

soil.detail: Regular potting mix with good moisture retention.

temp.detail: Dies back in winter, returns in spring.

seedDepth.detail: Seeds are tiny. Better to buy a small plant.

tips: Always grow in its own container. In the ground it spreads everywhere.
Cut back by half after flowering — regrows thicker with better flavour.
Repot every spring into fresh soil.
A pot near the door deters flies.
warning: Never plant mint in a garden bed. It spreads via underground runners and is near-impossible to contain.

name: Lavender
tagline: Drought-tolerant, beautiful, smells incredible. Bees love it and it barely needs attention.

water.detail: Let soil dry between waterings. Established lavender survives on rainfall.

sun.detail: More sun = more flowers.

soil.detail: Prefers poor soil. Rich compost causes lush growth but fewer flowers.

temp.detail: Wet roots in winter kill it faster than cold.

seedDepth.detail: Slow from seed. Easier to buy a small plant.

care.harvest: Cut when half the flowers are open

tips: Cut back by one-third after flowering every year. Never cut into old woody growth.
Number one killer is poor drainage. Plant in raised beds or add grit to soil.
Harvest when about half the tiny flowers are open.
Dried bundles in wardrobes repel moths naturally.
warning: Never let lavender sit in waterlogged soil in winter. Root rot will kill it quickly.

name: Rosemary
tagline: Mediterranean toughness. Barely needs watering, loves hot dry conditions, tastes incredible.

water.detail: Water when soil is dry 2–3cm down. Much less in winter.

sun.detail: Needs full sun. Grows poorly in shade.

soil.detail: Thrives in poor soil. Too much nutrition = soft growth prone to disease.

temp.detail: Good cold tolerance.

seedDepth.detail: Slow and unreliable from seed. Buy a plant.

tips: Prune after flowering each year to keep compact and bushy.
Take cuttings in summer — push 10cm stems into gritty compost.
Terracotta pot in full sun, barely watered — this is its ideal life.
The blue flowers are edible and beautiful scattered over salads.
warning: Overwatering and poor drainage kill rosemary fast. Black mushy stem base = root rot.

name: Thyme
tagline: One of the most useful kitchen herbs and one of the least demanding. A tiny plant lasts for years.

water.detail: Drought-tolerant. Prefers to dry out between waterings.

sun.detail: More sun = more intense flavour.

soil.detail: Sandy or gritty soil. Doesn't want rich compost.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy. Evergreen in mild winters.

seedDepth.detail: Seeds need light. Press onto surface and mist.

tips: Cut back by half in spring before new growth — prevents the woody, sprawling look.
The more you pick, the bushier it grows.
Lemon thyme is milder and citrusy — great if regular thyme is too strong.
Leave some flowers for bees — thyme is a favourite.
warning: Thyme hates wet heavy soil in winter. If it dies over winter, waterlogging is almost always the cause.

name: Chives
tagline: Basically unkillable. Perennial — plant once, harvest for years. The perfect beginner herb.

water.detail: Fairly drought-tolerant once established.

sun.detail: Grows in partial shade. Less demanding than most herbs.

soil.detail: Not fussy. Regular potting mix or garden soil both work.

temp.detail: Dies back in winter, reliably returns every spring.

seedDepth.detail: Scatter seeds thinly. Easier to buy a clump.

tips: Snip leaves 2–3cm from the base — they regrow continuously.
The purple flowers are edible and beautiful scattered on salads.
Divide the clump every 2–3 years in spring.
Grow near roses — chives are said to deter aphids.
warning: If you let it flower and set seed, it self-seeds prolifically. Deadhead if you don't want chives everywhere.

name: Parsley
tagline: Slow to start but then incredibly productive. One plant produces more than most families use.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist. Drying out weakens flavour.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade better than most herbs.

soil.detail: Unlike rosemary and thyme, parsley wants good rich soil.

temp.detail: Survives frosts. In year two it flowers and dies.

seedDepth.detail: Soak seeds overnight before sowing to speed germination.

tips: Patience — parsley is notoriously slow to germinate.
Harvest outer stems first, cutting close to the base.
Don't let it flower in year one — cut flower stems off immediately.
Flat-leaf (Italian) parsley is more flavourful; curly is more cold-hardy.
warning: In its second year it will flower and die — this is normal. Start fresh plants every spring.

name: Oregano
tagline: Drought-tolerant and intensely flavourful. The hotter and drier the conditions, the better it tastes.

water.detail: Let soil dry between waterings. Overwatering causes root rot and weak flavour.

sun.detail: Full sun = intense flavour. More sun = more aromatic oils.

soil.detail: Like most Mediterranean herbs, prefers lean, gritty soil.

temp.detail: Perennial in most climates. Dies back in winter, returns in spring.

seedDepth.detail: Seeds are tiny and need light. Press onto moist compost. Or buy a plant.

care.harvest: Just before flowering for best flavour

tips: Harvest just before the plant flowers — this is when the volatile oils (and flavour) are at their peak.
Cut back hard after flowering to keep it bushy and productive for next year.
Greek oregano is stronger than common oregano — use less of it.
Dry bundles hung upside down in a warm room — keeps the flavour for months.
warning: In heavy wet soil it tends to rot over winter. Plant in well-drained or raised beds.

name: Coriander / Cilantro
tagline: Incredibly versatile — both the leaves and seeds are used in cooking. But it bolts frustratingly fast.

water.detail: Keep moist but not waterlogged. Stress causes it to bolt.

sun.detail: Unlike most herbs, coriander actually does better with some shade in summer.

soil.detail: Not too rich. Light, free-draining compost mix.

temp.detail: Cool weather herb. Bolts almost immediately in heat.

seedDepth.detail: Sow direct — it hates being transplanted. The 'seed' is actually two seeds — both may sprout.

care.harvest: 3–4 weeks for leaves, 90 days for seeds

tips: Sow direct — coriander hates root disturbance and bolts immediately if transplanted.
Sow a small patch every 3 weeks for continuous leaf supply.
In summer, sow in afternoon shade and this significantly delays bolting.
If it bolts, let it flower and set seed — the seeds are just as useful in cooking and you get next year's supply.
warning: Bolts (runs to flower) extremely fast in heat or dry conditions. In summer, shade and regular watering are essential.

name: Sage
tagline: Beautiful, fragrant, drought-tolerant. The silver-green leaves look as good as they taste.

water.detail: Allow to dry between waterings. Overwatering is the main cause of failure.

sun.detail: Full sun produces the best flavour and most compact growth.

soil.detail: Sandy or gritty soil. Too rich = soft, disease-prone growth.

temp.detail: Very hardy once established. Evergreen in mild winters.

seedDepth.detail: Can be grown from seed but buying a plant is faster.

tips: Cut back by half in spring before new growth starts — keeps it compact and prevents it going woody.
Never cut into old brown woody stems — it won't regrow from there.
Purple sage and tricolour sage are ornamental varieties with the same flavour.
Exceptional with pork, chicken, and brown butter pasta.
warning: Sage can get leggy and woody if not pruned annually. Once very woody, it's often easier to start fresh with a new plant.

name: Dill
tagline: Fast-growing and feathery. Both the leaves and seeds are useful. Attracts beneficial insects.

water.detail: Keep moist. Inconsistent watering causes premature bolting.

sun.detail: Full sun essential. Weak in shade.

soil.detail: Not too rich. Light, sandy soil is ideal.

temp.detail: Bolts in heat. Sow spring and early autumn for best results.

seedDepth.detail: Sow direct — it hates being transplanted.

tips: Sow direct — dill hates root disturbance and goes to seed if transplanted.
Don't grow near fennel — they cross-pollinate and both lose their distinct flavour.
Leave some plants to flower and set seed — the seeds are just as valuable as the leaves.
Great companion plant for brassicas — attracts predatory wasps that eat caterpillars.
warning: Bolts quickly in heat. In summer, provide some afternoon shade or sow more frequently.

name: Strawberry
tagline: Perfect for balconies and small spaces. Grows in pots and hanging baskets. Fruit in the first year.

water.detail: Keep moist during fruiting. Water at the base — wet fruit rots.

sun.detail: More sun = sweeter fruit.

soil.detail: pH 5.5–6.5. Mix in compost.

temp.detail: Hardy in winter. Best fruit in warm days, cool nights.

seedDepth.detail: Buying starter plants is much easier than seed.

tips: Remove first flowers in year one — the plant roots deeper and gives far more fruit in years 2 and 3.
Runners can be pegged into small pots to make free new plants.
Lay straw under fruit to keep it off wet soil.
Net the plants once berries form — birds love them.
warning: Grey mould thrives in wet conditions. Space plants well and remove rotting fruit immediately.

name: Blueberry
tagline: Takes patience to establish but then produces fruit for 20+ years with minimal care.

water.detail: Needs consistent moisture, especially in the first 2 years.

sun.detail: Full sun essential for good fruit production.

soil.detail: Critical. Regular soil will slowly kill blueberries. Use ericaceous (acid) compost.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy. Needs cold winters to fruit well.

seedDepth.detail: Don't grow from seed. Plant 2+ varieties for better pollination.

tips: Always use ericaceous compost. Collect rainwater — tap water is often too alkaline.
Plant at least two varieties near each other for cross-pollination.
Prune out the oldest, darkest stems every winter.
Mulch with wood chip or pine needles to maintain soil acidity.
warning: Yellow leaves mean the soil pH is too high — they're starving for nutrients they can't access.

name: Raspberry
tagline: Once established, one of the most productive and low-maintenance fruits you can grow.

water.detail: Keep moist during fruiting. Mulch well to retain moisture.

sun.detail: Full sun for best yields. Tolerates light shade.

soil.detail: Dig in plenty of compost. Raspberries hate waterlogged soil.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy. Canes die back to the ground each year.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root canes in autumn or early spring, 5–8cm deep.

tips: After summer raspberries fruit, cut ALL the canes that fruited down to the ground. Leave the new green canes for next year.
Autumn varieties are even easier — cut all canes to the ground in February and they fruit on new growth.
Tie canes to wires or a frame — they flop without support and the fruit rots on the ground.
Mulch heavily with wood chip each spring to suppress weeds and retain moisture.
warning: Raspberry beetle grubs inside the fruit are common. Pick any white grubs out — they're harmless but unpleasant.

name: Apple Tree
tagline: A long-term investment that pays dividends for decades. Even a small garden can fit a dwarf apple tree.

water.detail: Water regularly for the first 2–3 years. Established trees are largely self-sufficient.

sun.detail: Full sun essential for fruiting and disease resistance.

soil.detail: Dig in compost when planting. Mulch annually around the base.

temp.detail: Needs cold winters for dormancy and spring frosts can kill blossoms.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root trees in autumn or winter when dormant.

tips: Choose a self-fertile variety (e.g. Cox, Braeburn, James Grieve) or plant two compatible varieties nearby.
Annual pruning in winter — remove crossing branches, dead wood, and the centre to create an open goblet shape.
Thin the fruitlets in June — reduce clusters to one or two fruits for larger, better-quality apples.
Dwarf or semi-dwarf rootstocks make management easy — M9 or M26 for small gardens.
warning: Codling moth and apple scab are the two main problems. Both are manageable with good hygiene — pick up fallen fruit, prune for airflow.

name: Lemon Tree
tagline: Possible even in cool climates in a pot. Flowers smell incredible and the fruit is a genuine achievement.

water.detail: Water when top 3–4cm of soil is dry. Never waterlogged. Use room-temperature water.

sun.detail: As much sun as possible. South-facing wall or greenhouse. Outdoors in summer only in cool climates.

soil.detail: Use citrus compost. Must drain fast — never sit in water.

temp.detail: Not frost-hardy. In cool climates: outside in summer, bright cool room in winter.

seedDepth.detail: Seed-grown lemons take 7–15 years to fruit. Buy a grafted tree for fruit in 2–3 years.

tips: Feed with citrus fertiliser every month from spring to autumn — yellowing leaves usually mean nutrient deficiency.
Move outside in late May when nights are above 10°C. Move back inside in September.
Inconsistent watering causes fruit drop. Keep moisture consistent.
Dry indoor air in winter causes leaf drop — mist the leaves occasionally or use a pebble tray.
warning: Scale insects (brown lumps on stems) and vine weevil are the main pests in indoor citrus. Check monthly and treat early.

name: Sunflower
tagline: The most satisfying seed-to-flower journey. A fingernail-sized seed becomes a plant taller than you.

water.detail: Water well when young. Established plants are surprisingly drought-tolerant.

sun.detail: They track the sun. They need as much as possible.

soil.detail: Not fussy at all. Grows in almost anything.

temp.detail: Grow fast in warm soil. Sow direct where they'll live.

seedDepth.detail: Sow direct — they hate being transplanted. One seed per hole.

tips: Sow directly where they'll grow — sunflowers hate root disturbance.
For giant single heads, remove side shoots. For multiple flowers, leave them.
Stake tall varieties early — staking a toppling sunflower is hard.
Leave the seed head after flowering for birds all autumn.
warning: Slugs eat seedlings overnight. Use copper ring or slug protection around young plants for the first few weeks.

name: Rose
tagline: More forgiving than their reputation. Modern varieties are tough, disease-resistant, and flower all summer.

water.detail: Deep watering at the base. Never overhead — wet leaves invite blackspot.

sun.detail: Less than 6 hours = fewer flowers and more disease.

soil.detail: Dig in lots of compost. Feed monthly during growing season.

temp.detail: Most modern roses are very cold-hardy.

seedDepth.value: Plant from bare root or pot
seedDepth.detail: Plant bare root roses in winter/early spring when dormant.

tips: Prune in early spring when you see new red buds — cut to an outward-facing bud at 45°.
Deadhead spent flowers throughout summer to trigger more blooms.
Feed with high-potassium feed after first flowering to fuel the next flush.
David Austin English roses combine old-fashioned scent with repeat-flowering.
warning: Blackspot (black spots on leaves, then yellowing) is common in wet summers. Remove affected leaves and avoid overhead watering.

name: Marigold
tagline: Cheerful, long-flowering, and one of the best companion plants in the garden. Repels pests naturally.

water.detail: Keep moist but not waterlogged. Drought-tolerant once established.

sun.detail: Full sun produces the most flowers. Tolerates partial shade.

soil.detail: Not fussy at all. Even poor soil works.

temp.detail: Half-hardy annual. Frost kills them.

seedDepth.detail: Easy from seed. Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or sow direct after frost.

tips: Plant near tomatoes, peppers, and courgettes — the scent deters aphids and whitefly.
Deadhead regularly to keep the flowers coming all summer.
Edible petals — sprinkle the orange and yellow petals over salads.
One of the easiest flowers to save seed from — let some flowers dry on the plant and collect the seeds.
warning: Slugs eat seedlings. Protect young plants. Once established they're much more resilient.

name: Nasturtium
tagline: Entirely edible, completely easy, and utterly cheerful. Flowers in poor soil where nothing else will.

water.detail: Drought-tolerant. Overwatering and rich soil causes lots of leaves and few flowers.

sun.detail: Full sun or partial shade both work.

soil.detail: This is key — rich soil produces leaves not flowers. Don't add compost.

temp.detail: Half-hardy annual. Frost kills them but they self-seed prolifically.

seedDepth.detail: Sow direct where they'll grow. Hate root disturbance.

tips: Entire plant is edible — flowers have a peppery flavour, seeds can be pickled like capers, young leaves go in salads.
Don't fertilise — really. Poor soil = more flowers. Rich soil = a sea of green leaves.
Aphids love them — use as a 'trap crop' planted near vegetables to draw aphids away.
Self-seeds generously — you'll likely never need to buy seeds again after the first year.
warning: Too much fertiliser or rich soil is the main reason for no flowers. If it's all leaves, stop feeding and move to poorer soil.

name: Geranium (Pelargonium)
tagline: The classic balcony and windowbox flower. Tough, long-flowering, and comes in an enormous range of colours.

water.detail: Water when top of soil is dry. They tolerate drought better than most flowering plants.

sun.detail: Full sun produces the most flowers. Tolerates light shade.

soil.detail: Standard potting compost with good drainage.

temp.detail: Bring indoors before first frost. Overwinter in a cool bright room.

seedDepth.detail: Take 10cm cuttings in late summer. Root in gritty compost. Easy and free.

tips: Deadhead regularly — remove old flowers with a sharp downward pull to prevent disease.
Take cuttings in August before the frosts — you'll have free plants for next year.
Overwintered plants bloom more profusely the following year than new plants from seed.
Remove yellow leaves immediately — they encourage botrytis (grey mould).
warning: Waterlogging kills them quickly. Always use pots with drainage holes and never let them sit in water.

name: Petunia
tagline: Spectacular trailing flowers all summer. Perfect for hanging baskets and window boxes.

water.detail: Hanging baskets especially dry out very fast. Water daily in heat.

sun.detail: Full sun essential for flowering. Poor in shade.

soil.detail: Good quality compost with slow-release fertiliser added.

temp.detail: Plant out after last frost. Flowers until first autumn frost.

seedDepth.detail: Tiny seeds need light to germinate. Press onto moist compost. Or buy plug plants.

tips: Feed every week with liquid tomato fertiliser — petunias are very hungry in containers.
Deadhead regularly or give the whole plant a trim when it gets straggly — it bounces back fast.
Sticky stems are normal for petunias — they trap insects as a minor carnivory adaptation.
Trailing varieties (Surfinia, Wave) are more vigorous and require less deadheading.
warning: Botrytis (grey mould) in wet weather can kill plants. Improve airflow and remove affected material immediately.

name: Lavender
tagline: Drought-tolerant, beautiful, smells incredible. Bees love it and it barely needs attention.

water.detail: Let soil dry between waterings. Established lavender survives on rainfall.

sun.detail: More sun = more flowers.

soil.detail: Prefers poor soil. Rich compost causes lush growth but fewer flowers.

temp.detail: Wet roots in winter kill it faster than cold.

seedDepth.detail: Slow from seed. Easier to buy a small plant.

care.harvest: Cut when half the flowers are open

tips: Cut back by one-third after flowering every year. Never cut into old woody growth.
Number one killer is poor drainage. Plant in raised beds or add grit to soil.
Harvest when about half the tiny flowers are open.
Dried bundles in wardrobes repel moths naturally.
warning: Never let lavender sit in waterlogged soil in winter. Root rot will kill it quickly.

name: Pansy
tagline: The cheerful face of winter and spring gardens. Thrives in cold when almost nothing else flowers.

water.detail: Keep moist. Drying out causes wilting and fewer flowers.

sun.detail: Cool-season plants that appreciate some afternoon shade in warmer months.

soil.detail: Good potting compost. Feed monthly.

temp.detail: Winter pansies are specifically bred for cold. Plant in autumn for colour all winter.

seedDepth.detail: Sow in summer for winter/spring flowering. Or buy plug plants.

tips: Deadhead regularly — removing old flowers dramatically extends the flowering season.
Plant autumn pansies in September for flowers through winter and into May.
The flowers are entirely edible — use to decorate cakes or salads.
They struggle in summer heat — replace with summer bedding plants in June.
warning: Slugs eat them enthusiastically in autumn. Protect with copper tape or slug pellets around containers.

name: Dahlia
tagline: The most spectacular late-summer flowers you can grow from tubers. Dramatic, long-lasting, and cut-flower perfect.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist once in full growth. Drought causes bud drop.

sun.detail: Full sun is essential for good flowers.

soil.detail: Dig in plenty of compost. Feed every 2 weeks with high-potassium fertiliser once budding starts.

temp.detail: Frost-sensitive. Plant tubers in spring, lift in autumn before hard frosts.

seedDepth.detail: Plant in spring when soil has warmed. Eye (growth bud) facing up.

tips: Pinch out the growing tip when the plant is 40cm tall — this produces more stems and more flowers.
Stake early — dahlias get top-heavy and snap in wind.
Cut flowers regularly — the more you cut, the more they produce.
In autumn, lift tubers after first frost blackens the foliage. Store in a cool dry place in barely damp compost.
warning: Slugs and snails devour emerging shoots in spring. Protect tubers with grit and check nightly for the first few weeks.

name: Blackhaw
tagline: A native North American shrub with four seasons of interest — spring flowers, berries, autumn colour, winter structure.

water.detail: Regular watering for the first 2 years. Drought-tolerant once mature.

sun.detail: Remarkably adaptable. Best flowering and berrying in full sun.

soil.detail: Tolerates clay, loam, or sandy soils.

temp.detail: Extremely cold-hardy native shrub.

seedDepth.detail: Difficult from seed. Buy a 1–2 year old plant from a nursery.

tips: Plant in autumn or early spring. Water well for the first two seasons.
Berries are edible and sweet after frost, used for jams and preserves.
Rarely needs pruning — only remove dead or crossing branches in late winter.
Attracts pollinators in spring and birds in autumn. Excellent wildlife plant.
warning: Young plants are slow to establish — don't give up if it looks dormant in year one.

name: Hydrangea
tagline: Spectacular large flower heads that last from summer to autumn and dry beautifully for indoor displays.

water.detail: Needs consistent moisture. Wilts dramatically when thirsty but recovers quickly.

sun.detail: Bright morning sun with afternoon shade is ideal. Full sun scorches the flowers.

soil.detail: Add plenty of compost. Acidic soil produces blue flowers; alkaline = pink.

temp.detail: Most are cold-hardy but late frosts can damage emerging flower buds.

seedDepth.detail: Easy from softwood cuttings in summer. Or buy a plant.

care.germination: N/A — buy plants or take cuttings

tips: Blue flowers = acidic soil. Pink flowers = alkaline soil. Add ericaceous compost or aluminium sulphate for blue.
Don't prune in autumn — leave old flowerheads on to protect next year's buds over winter. Prune in late spring.
Dead flowerheads look beautiful through winter and can be cut for dried arrangements.
Mulch heavily each spring to retain moisture — hydrangeas are thirsty.
warning: Late spring frosts kill emerging flower buds — avoid frost-prone spots or protect with fleece in late April.

name: Buddleia (Butterfly Bush)
tagline: The ultimate wildlife shrub. Cut it to the ground each spring and it regrows to 2m and flowers by July.

water.detail: Established plants survive on rainfall. Water only in extreme drought.

sun.detail: Full sun essential for best flowering.

soil.detail: Incredibly adaptable. Grows in poor rubble, chalk, or clay.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy once established.

seedDepth.detail: Self-seeds prolifically if not deadheaded. Can buy small plants cheaply.

tips: Cut down hard to 30cm from the ground every spring in March — it regrows vigorously and flowers on new wood.
Deadhead spent flower spikes to encourage more flowers and prevent aggressive self-seeding.
Attracts more butterfly species than almost any other garden plant — plant near a seating area.
White, pink, and dark purple varieties all available — mix for extended season.
warning: Invasive in some regions — check local regulations. Always deadhead before seeds set to prevent spread.

name: Wisteria
tagline: The most spectacular flowering climber. The waterfall of purple flowers in May is worth every bit of effort.

water.detail: Regular watering for the first 2–3 years. Drought-tolerant once established.

sun.detail: Full sun on a warm south or west-facing wall is ideal.

soil.detail: Not too rich in nitrogen — encourages leaves over flowers.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy. Late frosts can damage flower buds.

seedDepth.detail: Seed-grown plants can take 20 years to flower. Buy a named grafted variety.

care.harvest: Flowers May–June, sometimes again in August

tips: Buy a named grafted variety — not seed-grown. Check it was flowering when you bought it.
Prune twice a year: summer (July) — cut new shoots to 5 leaves. Winter — cut those same shoots to 2 buds.
If it won't flower: too much shade, too much nitrogen, or too young. Restrict the roots if vigorous but flowerless.
Tie in the structural branches when young — once woody they're impossible to redirect.
warning: All parts are toxic if eaten. Keep children away from the seeds especially — they look appealing.

name: English Oak
tagline: The most ecologically valuable tree you can plant. Supports over 500 species of insects, birds, and fungi.

water.detail: Water regularly for the first 2–3 years. After that, entirely self-sufficient.

sun.detail: Grows well in a wide range of light conditions.

soil.detail: Tolerates heavy clay, loam, or sandy soils. Deep soil preferred.

temp.detail: Extremely cold-hardy. Long-lived to 500–1000 years.

seedDepth.detail: Collect fresh acorns in autumn and plant immediately. Or buy a young tree.

care.harvest: Acorns from age 40–50. Timber in 100+ years.

tips: Plant as a gift to the future — even a 10-year-old oak is already a significant wildlife habitat.
Grow from acorns collected locally — locally-sourced genetics are better adapted to your conditions.
Small oaks (up to 1m) transplant better than larger ones. Plant young.
If space is limited, plant in a large container for the first 5–10 years.
warning: Mature size is enormous. Only plant in gardens large enough to accommodate a mature tree. Check for overhead cables.
